content.text: Promotion Approval Workflows and Audit Trail

A promotion is a decision to move an artifact from one environment to the next. In a GenAI system, that decision carries more weight than in a conventional service because a bad model version or prompt can produce subtly wrong outputs that are hard to detect and expensive to roll back. Promotion approval workflows make that decision explicit: a structured combination of automated evidence and human sign-off that must clear before deployment proceeds.

An audit trail records every promotion decision — including who approved, what evidence existed at the time, and (when policy was bypassed) why. Together, the workflow and the trail form the governance layer of your release pipeline.

attrs.alt: Promotion approval workflow stages and audit trail record fields

content.text: Why Automated Gates Are Not Enough

Automated eval gates catch regressions that have a measurable signal — accuracy below threshold, a safety rule triggered, a mutation score drop. But GenAI failures often fall outside what your current test suite covers:
A prompt change passes all existing evals but introduces a new failure mode on a topic category not yet covered by test cases.
A model update improves average accuracy but reduces calibration on low-frequency but high-stakes queries.
A retrieval change shifts the distribution of what the model sees without triggering any existing retrieval-quality probe.

Human reviewers read the eval summary, look at example outputs from the new version, consult the change description, and make a judgment call that incorporates context no test suite has been written to capture. The workflow's job is to surface that evidence clearly, not to replace human judgment.
The Two-Person Rule for Production Promotions
For production promotions, requiring two independent approvers — a team lead and a safety reviewer — prevents a single point of failure in the judgment chain. The rule has three properties:
Neither approver can be the author of the change being promoted.
Both approvals must arrive within the timeout window (72 hours). An expired window automatically rejects the promotion.
Approvals are tied to a specific artifact digest. Rebuilding the artifact invalidates all outstanding approvals.
GitHub Actions environment protection rules implement this directly. Set

# .github/environments/production.yml  (managed via Terraform or the UI)
# Environment: production
required_reviewers:
  - type: Team
    id: team-leads          # GitHub team slug
  - type: Team
    id: safety-reviewers
prevent_self_review: true
wait_timer: 30              # minutes — mandatory delay even after both approve
deployment_branch_policy:
  protected_branches: true  # only pushes to main can deploy
The
wait_timer

gives both approvers time to reconsider after the first approval lands. When either approver clicks Approve in the Actions UI, GitHub records their login and UTC timestamp. Your audit layer reads this record from the GitHub Deployments API.
Implementing the Approval Gate in CI
The approval gate sits between the final eval job and the deployment job. A summary job produces the evidence package that reviewers read before approving:

jobs:
  # --- automated phase ---
  eval-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      artifact_digest: ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
      eval_passed: ${{ steps.gate.outputs.passed }}
    steps:
      - id: build
        run: |
          docker build -t $REGISTRY/$IMAGE:$SHA .
          DIGEST=$(docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' $REGISTRY/$IMAGE:$SHA)
          echo "digest=$DIGEST" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - id: gate
        run: |
          python scripts/run_eval_gate.py \
            --digest ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }} \
            --output gate_result.json
          PASSED=$(jq -r '.passed' gate_result.json)
          echo "passed=$PASSED" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  gate-summary:
    needs: eval-gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: needs.eval-gate.outputs.eval_passed == 'true'
    steps:
      - name: Post gate summary to job log
        run: |
          python scripts/post_gate_summary.py \
            --digest '${{ needs.eval-gate.outputs.artifact_digest }}' \
            --results gate_result.json

  # --- human approval gate (environment protection rule) ---
  await-approval:
    needs: gate-summary
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production       # blocks here until 2 reviewers approve
    steps:
      - run: echo "Approval received. Proceeding to deploy."

  deploy-production:
    needs: await-approval
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy approved artifact
        run: |
          ./scripts/blue_green_deploy.sh \
            --digest '${{ needs.eval-gate.outputs.artifact_digest }}'
The
gate-summary

job prints the eval scores, the artifact digest, and a human-readable change summary to the workflow log. Reviewers can read this log directly in the Actions UI before clicking Approve. This is the evidence package.
Building the Append-Only Audit Log

Every promotion decision — approval, rejection, or bypass — produces one record appended to a JSON Lines file. The file is stored in object storage (S3, GCS) with object versioning and no-delete lifecycle policies to prevent tampering:

import hashlib, json, boto3, uuid
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def append_audit_record(
    artifact_digest: str,
    from_env: str,
    to_env: str,
    actor: str,
    decision: str,
    eval_scores: dict,
    justification: str,
    bypass: bool = False,
    cosign_bundle: str | None = None,
) -> dict:
    """Append one promotion decision to the immutable audit log."""
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    bucket = "myco-promotion-audit"
    key = f"audit/{to_env}/promotion.jsonl"

    # Read the current tail hash for chaining
    try:
        tail_obj = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key + ".tail")
        prev_hash = tail_obj["Body"].read().decode().strip()
    except s3.exceptions.NoSuchKey:
        prev_hash = "genesis"

    record = {
        "event_id": str(uuid.uuid4()),
        "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "actor": actor,
        "artifact_digest": artifact_digest,
        "from_env": from_env,
        "to_env": to_env,
        "eval_scores": eval_scores,
        "decision": decision,           # "approved" | "rejected" | "bypass"
        "bypass": bypass,
        "justification": justification,
        "cosign_bundle": cosign_bundle,
        "prev_hash": prev_hash,
    }
    record_json = json.dumps(record, sort_keys=True)
    record["log_hash"] = hashlib.sha256(record_json.encode()).hexdigest()

    # Append to JSONL file
    line = json.dumps(record) + "\n"
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket=bucket,
        Key=key,
        Body=line,
        ContentType="application/x-ndjson",
        # S3 versioning preserves every append; no-delete policy prevents removal
    )
    # Update tail pointer for next record's chain
    s3.put_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key + ".tail", Body=record["log_hash"])
    return record
The
prev_hash
field chains each record to the previous one. To verify the chain, walk the records in order and confirm that
SHA256(sorted_json_of_record_without_log_hash) == record['log_hash']
and that
record['prev_hash'] == log_hash_of_previous_record
. Any gap or mismatch indicates the log was tampered with.
Reading GitHub Approval Records Into the Audit Log

GitHub records who approved each environment deployment. The deploy job can read this record via the GitHub API and write it into your audit log, creating a traceable link from the workflow run to the audit entry:

# scripts/record_github_approval.py
import os, requests, json

def fetch_approval_actors(run_id: str, env_name: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Return the list of actors who approved this deployment run."""
    repo = os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"]
    token = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"}

    # List deployment reviews for this workflow run
    url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo}/actions/runs/{run_id}/approvals"
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
    resp.raise_for_status()

    approvals = resp.json()
    return [
        {
            "actor": a["user"]["login"],
            "approved_at": a["submitted_at"],
            "comment": a.get("comment", ""),
        }
        for a in approvals
        if a["state"] == "approved"
    ]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_id = os.environ["GITHUB_RUN_ID"]
    env_name = os.environ["TARGET_ENV"]
    artifact_digest = os.environ["ARTIFACT_DIGEST"]

    approvers = fetch_approval_actors(run_id, env_name)

    from audit_log import append_audit_record
    record = append_audit_record(
        artifact_digest=artifact_digest,
        from_env="staging",
        to_env=env_name,
        actor="; ".join(a["actor"] for a in approvers),
        decision="approved",
        eval_scores=json.loads(os.environ["EVAL_SCORES_JSON"]),
        justification="; ".join(a["comment"] for a in approvers if a["comment"]),
        cosign_bundle=os.environ.get("COSIGN_BUNDLE"),
    )
    print(json.dumps(record, indent=2))
Bypass Policy for Emergency Hotfixes
Every bypass must be harder, not easier, than the normal path. A bypass for a GenAI production deployment requires:
Condition
Requirement
Authorization
VP Eng + on-call engineer (different people)
Justification
Written incident ticket number in justification field
Post-incident
PIR within 48h; eval coverage gap added to test suite
Audit record
bypass=true + both authorizer logins logged
The bypass record itself is immutable — the audit log marks
"bypass": true

and captures the justification. Compliance dashboards surface bypass frequency as a key metric; rising bypass rates indicate the normal approval process is too slow and needs streamlining rather than being bypassed more often.
Querying the Audit Trail
JSON Lines format lets you query the audit trail with standard tools. Common compliance queries:

# All production promotions in the last 30 days
aws s3 cp s3://myco-promotion-audit/audit/production/promotion.jsonl - | \
  jq -c 'select(.timestamp > "2025-06-01T00:00:00Z")' | \
  jq '{artifact_digest, actor, decision, timestamp}'

# All bypasses (for compliance audit)
cat promotion.jsonl | jq -c 'select(.bypass == true)' | \
  jq '{event_id, timestamp, actor, justification, artifact_digest}'

# Verify the log chain (tamper detection)
python3 scripts/verify_audit_chain.py audit/production/promotion.jsonl

# scripts/verify_audit_chain.py
import hashlib, json, sys

def verify_chain(path: str) -> None:
    prev_hash = "genesis"
    with open(path) as f:
        for i, line in enumerate(f, 1):
            record = json.loads(line)
            stored_log_hash = record.pop("log_hash")
            stored_prev_hash = record["prev_hash"]

            if stored_prev_hash != prev_hash:
                print(f"CHAIN BREAK at record {i}: prev_hash mismatch")
                sys.exit(1)

            computed = hashlib.sha256(
                json.dumps(record, sort_keys=True).encode()
            ).hexdigest()
            if computed != stored_log_hash:
                print(f"TAMPER DETECTED at record {i}: hash mismatch")
                sys.exit(1)

            prev_hash = stored_log_hash

    print(f"Chain OK: {i} records verified")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    verify_chain(sys.argv[1])
Staging vs Production Gate Differences
Property
Staging Gate
Production Gate
Approvers required
1 (team lead)
2 (team lead + safety)
Wait timer
0 min
30 min
Timeout (auto-reject)
24 hours
72 hours
Eval score threshold
>=90% accuracy, >=80% mutation
>=95% accuracy, >=85% mutation
Bypass authorization
Team lead only
VP Eng + on-call engineer
Post-bypass review
Team retro
PIR within 48h
Alerting on Approval and Rejection Events
Route audit events to Slack and PagerDuty based on severity:
Common Pitfalls
Approval tied to a branch, not a digest.
If approval is granted and someone pushes another commit before deployment, the approved artifact is no longer what gets deployed. Always gate on digest, invalidate on rebuild.
Audit log in the same database as application state.
A database incident can corrupt audit history. Use separate append-only storage (object storage with versioning) that has its own access controls.
Bypass not audited differently from normal approvals.
Bypasses should stand out in dashboards. Mark them clearly and emit separate alerts — they represent exceptions that need post-incident review.
No timeout on pending approvals.
A promotion request that sits open for a week becomes ambiguous — the original eval evidence is stale. Set explicit timeouts and require re-triggering if the window expires.
Reviewers approving without reading the evidence.
The gate summary job must print a concise, human-readable summary directly in the workflow log. Reviewers who have to hunt for eval scores will rubber-stamp instead of reviewing.
Summary
Promotion approval workflows combine automated evidence (eval scores, mutation gate) with human sign-off, structured as GitHub environment protection rules.
The two-person rule for production — team lead plus safety reviewer, neither the change author — protects against single-point failures in human judgment.
Every decision is recorded in an append-only JSON Lines audit log with SHA-256 chain hashing for tamper evidence.
Approvals are tied to artifact digests and invalidated on rebuild, ensuring the approved artifact is the deployed artifact.
Bypass events trigger immediate alerts and mandatory post-incident review, making circumvention visible rather than silent.
